pairs.archetype_description: Four-axis anti-AI: GPT-impossible opener, obscure company reference, non-formula structure, front-loaded typo.
pairs.what_to_notice: Which email feels like it was written by a real person? Which one would you actually reply to?

version_a.subject_line: a question about bristol hospice's de novo expansion
version_a.email_body:
Hi Rachael,

Noticed Bristol Hospice just launched de novos across four states in January, with five more markets named for the next wave. Standing up new operations that fast usually means coding teams are the last group to scale, and clean claims from day one across Mississippi, Tulsa, and McAllen is its own problem.

One VP at a similar multi-state hospice network cut A/R days by 30% during their expansion quarter without adding coders.

VPs use autonomous coding to accelerate cash flow and keep accuracy above 98% as new locations come online. Worth 15 minutes to see if that approach maps to Bristol's launch cadence?

version_b.email_body:
Rachael, random Q, hows McAllen going.

Four states in one January is a lot. Mississippi, Pittsburgh, Tulsa, McAllen, plus the DaySpring acquisitions in Enterprise, Dothan, and Andalusia. The de novo VP job is basically building a plane mid-air and the coding team is always the last seat filled.

Most hospice expansions I see hit a wall at month four when the billing from the new sites lands and nothing codes clean. That's usually a cash flow problem nobody predicted.

Not a pitch, just an observation. Open to a 15 minute chat on what other multi-state hospices ran into.

pairs.archetype_description: 3-4 lines, ~50 words. Signal-led opener, what we do, the ask. LinkedIn-DM-short.
pairs.what_to_notice: Would the crisp 3-4 line version get a reply where the longer one gets ignored? Or does it feel too thin?

version_a.subject_line: a question about yale new haven's fiscal 2025 losses
version_a.email_body:
Hi Jennifer,

Saw reports that Yale New Haven Health closed fiscal 2025 down nearly $200 million, with Medicaid shortfalls over $430M and a new CFO just stepping in this March. Strategic operations at a five-hospital system carrying that kind of pressure tends to mean every dollar of coding accuracy matters more than it did 12 months ago.

One Director at a similar academic system cut denial rates 27% and recovered cash flow without adding to the billing team.

Directors use autonomous coding to bring coding costs down 40% while holding accuracy above 98%. Curious whether that math is worth a closer look for Yale New Haven's setup?

version_b.email_body:
Hi Jennifer, saw Yale New Haven closed fiscal 2025 down $200M with Medicaid shortfalls over $430M.

We help similar systems cut denial rates 27% and recover cash without growing the billing team.

Worth 15 minutes to see if it fits?

pairs.archetype_description: Hook sentence, 2-3 metric bullets, CTA sentence.
pairs.what_to_notice: Does the bulleted format make this easier or harder to read?

version_a.email_body:
Hi Kim,

Saw Holzer is a likely beneficiary of Ohio's $202M Rural Health Transformation Fund and just secured $999K for the lab modernization. Rural health dollars coming in is a good story, but Medicaid-heavy payer mix plus the nursing turnover climb from 33.7% to 41.8% usually means revenue cycle is carrying weight the balance sheet can't see yet.

One VP at a similar rural system unlocked $2.5M in cash flow by cleaning up coding before the capital money landed.

VPs use autonomous coding to cut A/R days 30% without adding headcount. Worth a short conversation about what that could mean for Holzer's fiscal 2026?

version_b.email_body:
Hi Kim,

Holzer sitting under Ohio's Rural Health Transformation dollars with nursing turnover climbing from 33.7% to 41.8% usually means revenue cycle is carrying more weight than the balance sheet shows.

What we've seen at similar rural systems:
- A/R days cut 30% in one quarter without adding coders
- Cash flow accelerated by $2.5M before capital projects land
- Coding accuracy held at 98%+ through nursing turnover spikes

Worth 15 minutes to see if that pattern applies to what you're navigating for fiscal 2026?

pairs.archetype_description: Anti-AI opener with an obscure reference and front-loaded typo vs a 3-line signal-led DM-short email. Which feels more human?
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
wasnt going to write but the 18 month restore on the 2021 Ryuk incident is still the longest one I have seen quoted publicly, Melanie.

rev cycle teams that live through a full PACS encryption event tend to carry coding scar tissue that doesnt show up on any dashboard, the queues just feel permanently off.

one Director at a similar specialty group recovered 40% of their coding cost after moving off the post incident workflow they thought they were stuck with.

if 2026 is the year to finally unwind that, I would love to be in the room.

version_b.email_body:
Melanie, the Castle Connolly #1 plus A-10 partnership year usually doubles coding volume before hiring can catch up.

We help ortho groups cut coding costs 40% while holding accuracy above 98%.

Open to 15 minutes?

pairs.archetype_description: Anti-AI typed-fast human vs the current THE ONE framework. Which voice earns the reply?
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
dont know if the S.E.E. Project funding loss is the one that hurts most Joanne, or if its one of the other three.

515 employees, a $50m January 2027 target, and finance running cover on all of it means the coding and billing side of the house is probably the last thing getting attention and the first thing leaking.

one VP at a similar sized nonprofit network found that 30% of their A/R days were sitting in coder reviewable claims nobody had cycles for.

if that is a useful conversation ahead of FY26, I am around.

version_b.subject_line: a question about mar monte's fy26 setup
version_b.email_body:
Hi Joanne,

Saw Planned Parenthood Mar Monte is losing $6.5m a month in reimbursable care while standing up a $50m raise target by January 2027, with California's $90m state backfill only partially covering the federal cuts. Finance teams running that kind of double pressure usually find the quietest leaks in the coding and billing workflow.

One VP at a similar multi site nonprofit network cut denial rates 27% without adding a coder.

VPs use autonomous coding to hold accuracy above 98% while cost to collect comes down 70%. Worth 15 minutes to see if that would apply to Mar Monte?

pairs.archetype_description: 3-line signal-crisp DM vs an anonymized peer story. Brevity or narrative?
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
Kelly, $110m operating loss plus new Oregon wage contracts means rev cycle is carrying more weight in FY26.

We help academic systems cut cost to collect 70% while holding accuracy above 98%.

Open to 15 minutes?

version_b.subject_line: how a peer academic system ran fy after the downgrade
version_b.email_body:
Hi Kelly,

A VP of Enterprise Rev Cycle at a similar academic system walked me through their first full fiscal year after an S&P downgrade.

They assumed the fix was a hiring freeze and a vendor consolidation. Once they audited the claim level data, coding accuracy was sitting at 94%, which is fine on a dashboard and expensive on a P&L. After moving onto autonomous coding, accuracy went to 98% plus, A/R days came down 30%, and cost to collect dropped 70%, all inside the same labor contract cycle.

OHSU's FY26 setup looks like the same starting point.

Worth 15 minutes?

pairs.archetype_description: Anti-AI human vs signal-crisp, second signal pairing for variance.
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
wasnt sure if Christy taking over as chair was supposed to be a quiet handoff or the start of something, Jessica.

either way, revenue integrity teams inherit most of what a new finance chair wants to clean up first. Nashville General's audit note plus Tennessee still holding out on Medicaid expansion is an awkward baseline to open FY26 on.

one peer hospital in a non-expansion state found 27% of their denials were coder-preventable, not payer-preventable, which flipped how they budgeted.

happy to walk through that if it is useful before the FY26 plan locks.

version_b.email_body:
Jessica, non expansion states are absorbing more self pay and charity volume, which hits revenue integrity first.

We help hospitals cut denial rates 27% and hold accuracy above 98%.

Worth a 15 minute look?
